sentence: By the time we arrived, the concert already started.
correct: By the time we arrived, the concert had already started.
explanation: Use past perfect <had started> for an action completed before another past action.

sentence: She has been living in London since five years.
correct: She has been living in London for five years.
explanation: Use <for> with a duration of time, <since> with a point in time.

sentence: He made a big fault in his calculations.
correct: He made a big mistake in his calculations.
He made a big error in his calculations.
explanation: Collocation: we say <make a mistake/error>, not <make a fault>.

sentence: I am knowing the answer to this question.
correct: I know the answer to this question.
explanation: <Know> is a stative verb and is not used in continuous tenses.

sentence: She did a lot of progress in her English studies.
correct: She made a lot of progress in her English studies.
explanation: Collocation: we say <make progress>, not <do progress>.

sentence: When I will arrive, I will call you.
correct: When I arrive, I will call you.
explanation: After <when> in future time clauses, use present simple, not <will>.

sentence: He has gone to Paris last week.
correct: He went to Paris last week.
explanation: Use past simple (not present perfect) with specific past time expressions like <last week>.

sentence: She does always her homework before dinner.
correct: She always does her homework before dinner.
explanation: Adverbs of frequency go before the main verb: <always does>.

sentence: I am used to get up early every morning.
correct: I am used to getting up early every morning.
explanation: <Be used to> is followed by a gerund (-ing): <used to getting up>.

sentence: He suggested me to apply for the job.
correct: He suggested that I apply for the job.
He suggested I should apply for the job.
explanation: <Suggest> is followed by <that + subject + base verb>, not <someone to do>.

sentence: By next year, I will be working here since ten years.
correct: By next year, I will have been working here for ten years.
explanation: Use future perfect continuous with <for> (duration), not <since>.

sentence: She said me that she was tired.
correct: She told me that she was tired.
explanation: Use <tell> (not <say>) when there is an indirect object: <told me>.

sentence: I have been to the dentist yesterday.
correct: I went to the dentist yesterday.
explanation: Use past simple with <yesterday>, not present perfect.

sentence: He did a decision to change his career.
correct: He made a decision to change his career.
explanation: Collocation: we say <make a decision>, not <do a decision>.

sentence: She is believing that everything will be fine.
correct: She believes that everything will be fine.
explanation: <Believe> is a stative verb and is not used in continuous tenses.

sentence: I have seen that film three times since I was a child.
correct: I have seen that film three times since I was a child.
explanation: This sentence is actually correct — present perfect with <since> and a number of times.

sentence: He made an effort to do his best at the exam.
correct: He made an effort to do his best in the exam.
explanation: Collocation: we say <in the exam>, not <at the exam>.

sentence: Before she left, she has already packed her bags.
correct: Before she left, she had already packed her bags.
explanation: Use past perfect <had packed> for an action before another past action.

sentence: I am not agreeing with your opinion.
correct: I do not agree with your opinion.
I don't agree with your opinion.
explanation: <Agree> is a stative verb and is not used in continuous tenses.

sentence: She did a phone call to her mother.
correct: She made a phone call to her mother.
explanation: Collocation: we say <make a phone call>, not <do a phone call>.

sentence: After I will finish my homework, I will watch TV.
correct: After I finish my homework, I will watch TV.
explanation: After <after> in future time clauses, use present simple, not <will>.

sentence: He has been studying English for he was ten years old.
correct: He has been studying English since he was ten years old.
explanation: Use <since> with a point in time, not <for>.

sentence: She did a complaint about the service.
correct: She made a complaint about the service.
explanation: Collocation: we say <make a complaint>, not <do a complaint>.

sentence: I am owning a car since 2020.
correct: I have owned a car since 2020.
explanation: <Own> is a stative verb — use present perfect, not continuous.

sentence: He told that he would come to the party.
correct: He said that he would come to the party.
explanation: Use <say> (not <tell>) when there is no indirect object.
